sections.summary: Document heading: Standard for Pineapples (CXS 182-1993) with adoption in 1993, revision in 1999 and amendments in 2005 and 2011.

sections.section: CXS 182-1993 Adopted in 1993. Revised in 1999. Amended in 2005, 2011.
sections.summary: Document heading repeated: Standard for Pineapples (CXS 182-1993) with adoption in 1993, revision in 1999 and amendments in 2005 and 2011.
Defines scope: applies to commercial varieties of fresh pineapples (Ananas comosus) supplied to consumers after preparation and packaging and excludes pineapples destined for industrial processing.

Sets overall quality provisions: in all classes pineapples must be whole, sound, clean, practically free of pests and visible foreign matter, free of abnormal external moisture and foreign odour/taste, fresh in appearance, free of internal browning and pronounced blemishes, with peduncle ≤2.0 cm and clean cut, must be physiologically ripe and able to withstand transport; it also specifies maturity (see 2.1.2) and that pineapples are classified into three classes with class‑specific defect tolerances.

Repeats the minimum requirements: pineapples must be whole, sound, clean, practically free of pests and foreign matter, free of abnormal moisture and off‑odours, fresh in appearance, free of internal browning and pronounced blemishes, peduncle (if present) ≤2.0 cm with a clean transverse cut, and the fruit must be physiologically ripe; maturity is assessed by total soluble solids (see 2.1.2).

sections.section: 2.1.1 The pineapples have reached an appropriate degree of development and ripeness in accordance with criteria proper to the variety and/or commercial type and to the area in which they are grown.
sections.summary: Requires appropriate development and ripeness for the variety/commercial type and production area, and that pineapples be in condition to withstand transport and handling and arrive in satisfactory condition at destination.
Maturity requirement: total soluble solids in the flesh should be at least 12°Brix, determined from a representative sample of the juice of all the fruit.
Heading only: introduces classification of pineapples into three classes (details follow in subsequent sections).

'Extra' class criteria: pineapples must be of superior quality, characteristic of the variety/commercial type and free of defects except very slight superficial ones that do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation; if the crown is present it must be simple, straight, without sprouts and between 50–150% of fruit length for untrimmed crowns.

Class I criteria: pineapples must be of good quality and characteristic of the variety/commercial type, with slight allowed defects (shape, colouring including sun spots, and skin defects up to 4% of surface) that do not affect the pulp or the fruit's keeping quality or presentation; crowns, if present, must be simple and straight or slightly curved with no sprouts and between 50–150% of fruit length for trimmed or untrimmed crowns.

Class II pineapples are those that do not qualify for higher classes but meet the minimum requirements; allowed defects include shape and colouring faults (including sun spots) and skin defects (scratches, scars, scrapes, bruises, blemishes) not exceeding 8% of total surface area, provided the pulp is unaffected, and the crown, if present, shall be simple or double, straight or slightly curved, with no sprouts.

Size is determined by average fruit weight with a minimum of 700 g (250 g for small varieties), and size codes A–H specify average weights (±12%) with and without crown; exporters commonly pack by count into boxes sized to meet market minimum weights (e.g., 10 kg, 20 lbs, 40 lbs).

Inspection lots may include tolerances for quality and size: quality tolerances permit specified percentages of fruit not meeting the declared class (see 4.1), and size tolerances allow 10% by number or weight of fruit one size above or below the size indicated on the package.

Quality tolerances permit limited proportions of non-conforming fruit by class: 'Extra' allows 5% not meeting the class but meeting Class I (or within that class's tolerances), Class I allows 10% not meeting the class but meeting Class II (or within that class's tolerances), and Class II allows 10% not meeting either the class or minimum requirements except for fruit affected by rotting or other deterioration rendering it unfit for consumption.
For the 'Extra' class, up to 5% by number or weight of pineapples may fail to meet the class requirements provided they meet Class I standards or, exceptionally, fall within that class's tolerances.
For Class I, up to 10% by number or weight of pineapples may fail to meet the class requirements provided they meet Class II standards or, exceptionally, fall within that class's tolerances.

For Class II, up to 10% by number or weight of pineapples may fail to meet either the class requirements or the minimum requirements, except that fruit affected by rotting or other deterioration making them unfit for consumption are excluded.
Size tolerances for all classes permit 10% by number or weight of pineapples to correspond to the size immediately above or below the size indicated on the package.

Presentation provisions require package contents to be uniform by origin, variety/commercial type, quality and size (with 'Extra' class also requiring uniform colour and ripeness), that the visible contents represent the whole, and that packing materials and containers protect the fruit, be clean/food-grade and comply with the Code of Practice for Packaging and Transport of Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 44-1995).

Each package must be uniform and contain only pineapples of the same origin, variety/commercial type, quality and size, with the visible portion representative of the whole; for 'Extra' class colour and ripeness must also be uniform.

Pineapples must be packed to protect the produce using new, clean, food-grade materials (recycled food-grade allowed), printing/labels must use non-toxic inks/glues, and packing must comply with the Code of Practice CXC 44-1995.
Containers must meet requirements for quality, hygiene, ventilation and strength to ensure proper handling, shipping and preservation, and packages must be free of foreign matter and odour.

Marking and labelling provisions add specific requirements to the General Standard for Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CXS 1-1985): consumer packages and non-retail containers must include particulars such as produce name (and variety optionally), absence of crown if applicable, exporter/packer identification, country/district of origin, class, size (or average weight), optional unit count and net weight, and may bear an official inspection mark.
Consumer package labelling must meet the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CXS 1-1985) and additional specific provisions set out in this standard.

If the produce is not visible from the outside, each consumer package must be labelled with the name of the produce and may include the variety/commercial type, and the absence of the crown should be indicated.
Non-retail containers must bear legible, indelible particulars grouped on the same side and visible from the outside or be documented in accompanying shipment papers.
Non-retail container identification must include the name and address of the exporter, packer and/or dispatcher, with an optional identification code.
Non-retail containers must state the name of the produce if contents are not visible, may include variety/commercial type, and should indicate the absence of the crown when applicable.
Non-retail containers must indicate the country of origin and may optionally specify the district or national/regional/local place name where grown.
Commercial identification on non-retail containers must show the class and size (size code or average weight in grams) and may optionally include number of units and net weight.
Heading only: title indicates an optional official inspection mark but contains no substantive provisions or requirements.

Requires the produce covered by the Standard to comply with maximum levels in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) and with Codex-established maximum residue limits for pesticides.

Recommends that the produce be prepared and handled according to Codex hygienic texts (CXC 1-1969, CXC 53-2003 and other relevant Codes), and states the produce should comply with any microbiological criteria established per CXG 21-1997.
